paragraphs.body: Character is the Heart of Fiction

Character is what makes stories come to life and draws the audience in, inviting them to care beyond a surface level about what happens at the end. Through the readings I have done in the course as well as various drafts of getting to know characters of my own creation, I have come to understand how character operates within a story and its irreplaceable importance. Of course, writing fiction comes with a plethora of other elements that come together to make a story worthwhile: setting, plot, conflict, but every single one of these elements exist to make the readers better understand the character they are reading about. Character allows the audience to see themselves in the story and relate to the struggles and conflict the narrative presents. My opinion about character’s importance may also be slightly biased because it is the element of fiction that I find myself latching onto the most. I often enjoy a story in a book, movie, or short story that is “about nothing” or seemingly lacking in plot, but instead takes its time be a character study, fully fleshing a person out and making them into someone I feel like I know personally and understand. On the other hand, if a story with a very compelling plot is taken on by a character without much to them that simply reacts rather than taking action, I don’t connect with the story.

paragraphs.body: I believe that character is oftentimes perceived as the backbone of fiction because it is what separates the genre from nonfiction. Though the people present in nonfiction works are characters in their own right, authors have much less control over them, making the pieces far more driven by conflict. Prior to taking this class, I was much more well-versed in academic non-fiction writing, where the characters are defined by history and their actions have already been taken, leaving analyzing and interpreting the implications of those actions as the only thing left to do. With fiction, however, I have found the process to be much more freeing and complicated simultaneously. For me, writing my stories always starts with the character, and everything else builds around them. In my own work, when I had to begin a story, I did not first think about what I wanted to happen, rather I thought about who I wanted it to happen to. I began most stories by asking myself what kind of person I wanted to write about, how similar I wanted them to be to myself, and what I think their values would be. Realistic fiction borrows from the real world of course, in terms of setting and real-world problems, but the characters always begin as blank slates. The hardest part for me was trying to make sure that my characters felt like real people and that they were not just alternate iterations of myself. I know that it is extremely common for first time authors to write self-insert characters, sometimes without even realizing, so I tried to write my characters to feel like unique people that I might not even agree with or be friends with if they were real.

annotations.comment: Hmmm, yes, somewhat. Character is indeed important in some NF, but I agree that the author can do less to shape it.

paragraphs.body: To delve into what character can do to build a reader’s investment in a story, I chose to read “This is Pleasure” by Mary Gaitskill. The story is entirely built off character, with the story focusing on the actions and personality of one man, Quin, both through his own perception and opinion of himself and through the eyes of his friend Margot. Compelling characters are what make a story stand out to me, and Quin is particularly interesting because he bases his entire sense of self on his ability to understand and predict the actions and backgrounds of the characters around him, while failing to understand himself at the same time. Despite having a wife and child, Quin boasts about having a revolving door of women that he flirts with and tries to get in the head of and “figure them out,” treating it almost like a game to mess with them and then never call them again. Upon reading this story for the first time, I deeply disliked Quin, which is another element of character that I think is a difficult and impressive feat for an author to achieve. The way he treated women for the duration of the story was inexcusable and unfortunately rang true for me and probably many other female readers. I especially liked Gaitskill’s choice to place this story for a lot of the time through the eyes of Margot, Quin’s friend and colleague. Seeing her reflect on her interactions with him and reckon with what she did or didn’t do to curb his uncomfortable behavior allowed me to understand her more as a character and understand how she interpreted her position as a woman dealing with a man in a position of power over her. Gaitskill’s choice to flesh out the characters through multiple perspectives, intermittent flashbacks, and quite a lot of internal reflection is something that I was inspired by and decided to implement in a lot of my own writing because I believe it is one of the best ways to understand a character the way they are understood in their own world and others’ worlds.

paragraphs.body: While writing my two short stories, I tried to focus on character the most because making the audience care about your protagonist in under two thousand words is the most difficult part. What helped me the most was using multiple perspectives, like in my story “Forgiveness,” where I swapped perspectives between the two girlfriends, allowing the readers to understand how the characters viewed themselves and their internal motivations as well as how they were perceived by the person that was supposedly closest to them. In my second short story “Right Where You Left Me,” my intention was to let the reader inside the mind of one particularly troubled character who has found herself frozen in time following a hard breakup. Setting the story almost entirely in her head and allowing the reader to process what is happening to her along with her was helpful to me as an author because it helped me get to know her personality as I was writing. As an amateur author, I also found that emulating characters based on people I know in my real life, whether they were strangers I saw at the bus station or old friends, was helpful because it informed their motivations and reactions to certain conflicts.

annotations.comment: I concur! This feels like it’s lacking a conclusion, but is otherwise a strong paper!
